content.text: What Management Groups Are

Azure Management Groups are containers that sit above subscriptions in the Azure resource hierarchy. They exist for one reason: to apply Azure Policy and Azure RBAC role assignments at a scope that spans multiple subscriptions simultaneously. Without management groups, you would have to apply policies and roles to each subscription individually — a maintenance burden that grows with every new subscription.

Management groups form a tree. At the root is the Tenant Root Group, which contains all subscriptions and management groups in the Azure Active Directory tenant. Below the root you create management groups to reflect your organizational structure, and below those you place subscriptions. Every subscription belongs to exactly one management group. Policy and RBAC assigned at a management group scope inherit down to all management groups and subscriptions below it.

attrs.alt: Azure Management Group hierarchy: Tenant Root Group at top, Corp/Platform/Sandbox management groups at level 1, department sub-groups at level 2, and subscriptions at the leaf level

content.text: The Azure Resource Hierarchy
Management groups sit at the top of the four-level Azure resource hierarchy:
Level
Scope
Contains
Policy/RBAC applies to
Management Group
Tenant-wide container
Subscriptions and child management groups
All subscriptions and resources below it
Subscription
Billing and access boundary
Resource groups
All resource groups and resources in the subscription
Resource Group
Lifecycle container
Resources
All resources in the resource group
Resource
Individual service instance
N/A (leaf)
The specific resource

A policy or RBAC assignment at a higher scope applies to every item below it. A deny policy at the management group level cannot be overridden by an allow at the subscription level — the deny wins. This makes management groups the correct place for non-negotiable governance rules that must apply across the entire organization.
The Tenant Root Group

Every Azure Active Directory tenant has exactly one Tenant Root Group created automatically when the first subscription is added. All subscriptions and management groups in the tenant are children of this root, directly or indirectly. Assigning a policy or RBAC role at the Tenant Root Group scope applies it to every subscription in the tenant.

By default, only the Global Administrator of the Azure Active Directory tenant has access to the Tenant Root Group. Other administrators must be explicitly granted Tenant Root Group access before they can create child management groups or assign policies there.

# Elevate access to manage the Tenant Root Group (requires Global Admin in AAD)
# This grants the current user the User Access Administrator role at root scope
az rest --method post \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/elevateAccess?api-version=2016-07-01"

# Verify management group hierarchy
az account management-group list --output table

# Show the Tenant Root Group ID (use 'tenantId' as the group name)
az account management-group show \
  --name "$(az account show --query tenantId -o tsv)" \
  --expand --recurse

# After elevation, create a child management group
az account management-group create \
  --name "Corp" \
  --display-name "Corporate" \
  --parent "$(az account show --query tenantId -o tsv)"

# Move a subscription into a management group
az account management-group subscription add \
  --name "Corp" \
  --subscription "00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001"

Elevating access to the Tenant Root Group grants broad permissions. Remove the elevation after completing root-level configuration: az role assignment delete --role 'User Access Administrator' --scope '/' --assignee <your-upn>
Designing the Management Group Hierarchy

The management group hierarchy design determines where policies and roles are assigned and how much inheritance each scope receives. Azure limits the hierarchy to six levels of nesting below the root (not counting the root itself or subscriptions). Most organizations need two to three levels.
Two common hierarchy patterns:
Enterprise-scale (Microsoft CAF Landing Zone pattern)
Root: Tenant Root Group
Level 1: Corp (workloads), Platform (shared services), Sandbox (experiments), Decommissioned (retired subs)
Level 2 under Corp: Production, Non-Production; or by business unit (Finance, Engineering, HR)
Level 2 under Platform: Connectivity (hub VNet, DNS, ExpressRoute), Identity (AAD Domain Services, Key Vault), Management (monitoring, Security Center)
Subscriptions assigned to the appropriate level-2 group based on workload type
Flat (small organization pattern)
Root: Tenant Root Group
Level 1: Production, Non-Production, Sandbox
Subscriptions assigned directly to one of those three groups

The flat pattern is simpler but loses the ability to apply different policies to different business units at the management group level. The Enterprise-scale pattern requires more upfront design but scales better as the organization grows.

# Create an Enterprise-scale management group structure
TENANT=$(az account show --query tenantId -o tsv)

# Level 1 groups
az account management-group create --name "Corp" --display-name "Corporate" --parent "$TENANT"
az account management-group create --name "Platform" --display-name "Platform" --parent "$TENANT"
az account management-group create --name "Sandbox" --display-name "Sandbox" --parent "$TENANT"
az account management-group create --name "Decommissioned" --display-name "Decommissioned" --parent "$TENANT"

# Level 2 under Corp
az account management-group create --name "Corp-Prod" --display-name "Corp Production" --parent "Corp"
az account management-group create --name "Corp-NonProd" --display-name "Corp Non-Production" --parent "Corp"

# Level 2 under Platform
az account management-group create --name "Platform-Connectivity" --display-name "Connectivity" --parent "Platform"
az account management-group create --name "Platform-Identity" --display-name "Identity" --parent "Platform"
az account management-group create --name "Platform-Management" --display-name "Management" --parent "Platform"

# Verify structure
az account management-group list --query "[].{name:name, displayName:displayName, parent:details.parent.displayName}" --output table
Assigning Azure Policy at the Management Group Scope

Azure Policy assignments at the management group scope apply to all subscriptions and resource groups under that management group. This is the primary use case for management groups: enforcing compliance rules across a fleet of subscriptions without configuring each subscription individually.

# Assign the built-in policy "Allowed locations" at the Corp management group scope
# This restricts all Corp subscriptions to deploy resources only in specified regions
az policy assignment create \
  --name "allowed-locations-corp" \
  --display-name "Allowed locations for Corp" \
  --policy "e56962a6-4747-49cd-b67b-bf8b01975c4f" \
  --scope "/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/Corp" \
  --params '{"listOfAllowedLocations": {"value": ["eastus", "westus2", "northeurope"]}}'

# Assign a custom policy initiative (policy set) at the Tenant Root Group scope
# (Applies to ALL subscriptions in the tenant)
az policy set-definition create \
  --name "baseline-governance" \
  --display-name "Baseline Governance Initiative" \
  --definitions '[
    {"policyDefinitionId": "/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions/06a78e20-9358-41c9-923c-fb736d382a4d"},
    {"policyDefinitionId": "/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions/9daedab3-fb2d-461e-b861-71790eead4f6"}
  ]'

az policy assignment create \
  --name "baseline-governance-root" \
  --policy-set-definition "baseline-governance" \
  --scope "/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/$(az account show --query tenantId -o tsv)"

# Check compliance across all subscriptions for a management group assignment
az policy state summarize \
  --management-group "Corp" \
  --query "{compliant:results.compliantResources, nonCompliant:results.nonCompliantResources}" \
  --output table
Assigning RBAC Roles at the Management Group Scope

RBAC role assignments at the management group scope grant the assigned principal access to all subscriptions and resources under that management group. This is useful for cross-subscription roles: a central security team that needs Reader access across all production subscriptions, or a billing administrator who needs Cost Management access across the entire tenant.

# Assign the Reader role to a security team at the Corp management group scope
# This grants read access to all resources in all Corp subscriptions
az role assignment create \
  --role "Reader" \
  --assignee "security-team@contoso.com" \
  --scope "/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/Corp"

# Assign Cost Management Reader to a finance group at the Tenant Root scope
az role assignment create \
  --role "Cost Management Reader" \
  --assignee-object-id "$(az ad group show --group 'Finance Team' --query id -o tsv)" \
  --assignee-principal-type Group \
  --scope "/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/$(az account show --query tenantId -o tsv)"

# List all role assignments at management group scope (not inherited)
az role assignment list \
  --scope "/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/Corp" \
  --query "[].{role:roleDefinitionName, principal:principalName, type:principalType}" \
  --output table

# List all effective role assignments including inherited (shows what a principal can do)
az role assignment list \
  --scope "/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/Corp" \
  --include-inherited \
  --query "[].{role:roleDefinitionName, principal:principalName, scope:scope}" \
  --output table

Management group RBAC assignments cannot be scoped to exclude a child management group. If you need to give a principal access to Platform subscriptions but not Corp subscriptions, assign the role at the Platform management group scope, not at the root.
Moving Subscriptions Between Management Groups

Subscriptions can be moved between management groups at any time. Moving a subscription causes it to immediately inherit the policies and RBAC assignments of its new parent management group, and to lose the policies and RBAC assignments of its previous parent (unless those assignments also exist at a higher level it still belongs to).

Moving a subscription does not affect the resources inside the subscription. Existing Azure resources continue to exist; the change is purely governance scope — which policies and RBAC assignments apply.

# Move a subscription from Corp-NonProd to Sandbox
SUBSCRIPTION_ID="00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001"

# Remove from current management group
az account management-group subscription remove \
  --name "Corp-NonProd" \
  --subscription "$SUBSCRIPTION_ID"

# Add to target management group
az account management-group subscription add \
  --name "Sandbox" \
  --subscription "$SUBSCRIPTION_ID"

# Verify new location
az account management-group show \
  --name "Sandbox" \
  --expand \
  --query "children[?name=='$SUBSCRIPTION_ID'].{name:displayName, id:name}"

# Check what policies now apply to the subscription (after move)
az policy state list \
  --subscription "$SUBSCRIPTION_ID" \
  --query "[?complianceState=='NonCompliant'].{policy:policyAssignmentName, resource:resourceId}" \
  --output table
Limits and Constraints
Management groups have a set of hard limits that affect hierarchy design:
Constraint
Limit
Design implication
Max nesting depth
6 levels below root (not counting root or subscriptions)
Most organizations need 2–3 levels; 6 gives ample room
Max management groups per tenant
Not a practical constraint for most organizations
Subscription parent
Exactly 1 (each subscription belongs to exactly one MG)
Subscriptions cannot span management groups
Max policy assignments per scope
Initiatives (policy sets) count as one; prefer initiatives over many individual assignments
Max RBAC assignments per scope
2,000 at subscription level; no specific MG limit but total AAD limit applies
Use groups rather than individual user assignments to stay within limits
Inherited vs. Directly Assigned Policies
Understanding the difference between an inherited policy and a directly assigned policy is essential for diagnosing compliance issues:
Inherited:

the policy assignment was made at a parent scope (management group or subscription above the current scope). The policy is visible at the child scope but the assignment cannot be modified there — you must modify it at the scope where it was assigned.
Directly assigned:
the policy assignment was made at the current scope (this management group, this subscription, this resource group, or this resource). You can modify or remove it at this scope.

Exemptions allow you to exclude a specific resource or resource group from a policy that is otherwise inherited. An exemption does not remove the policy assignment — it creates a record at the exempted scope that instructs the policy evaluation engine to skip that scope.

# Create a policy exemption for a specific resource group
# (Exempts the resource group from a policy inherited from a management group)
az policy exemption create \
  --name "exempt-dev-rg" \
  --display-name "Exempt dev resource group from allowed-locations policy" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/SUB_ID/resourceGroups/dev-rg" \
  --policy-assignment "/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/Corp/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyAssignments/allowed-locations-corp" \
  --exemption-category Waiver \
  --expiration-date "2025-12-31"

# List all exemptions in a subscription
az policy exemption list \
  --scope "/subscriptions/SUB_ID" \
  --output table

# Check effective policy assignments on a resource group (includes inherited)
az policy assignment list \
  --resource-group dev-rg \
  --disable-scope-strict-match \
  --query "[].{name:name, scope:scope, policy:policyDefinitionId}" \
  --output table
Management Groups in Bicep and Terraform

Infrastructure-as-code tools support management group resource creation and policy/RBAC assignment at management group scope. This enables version-controlled governance infrastructure — the management group hierarchy and its policy assignments are defined in code and deployed via CI/CD pipelines.
Auditing and Monitoring Management Group Configuration

Changes to management group configuration (creating groups, moving subscriptions, policy assignments, RBAC assignments) are recorded in the Azure Activity Log. Because management groups are tenant-level resources, the Activity Log entries appear under the subscription of the user who made the change, or can be queried at the management group level.

# View recent management group operations in the Activity Log
az monitor activity-log list \
  --namespace "Microsoft.Management" \
  --offset 48h \
  --query "[].{time:eventTimestamp, operation:operationName.localizedValue, caller:caller, status:status.value}" \
  --output table

# Check policy compliance summary across all subscriptions under Corp
az policy state summarize \
  --management-group "Corp" \
  --query "{compliantResources:results.compliantResources, nonCompliantResources:results.nonCompliantResources, totalResources:results.resourceDetails[0].count}"

# Export management group structure as JSON (useful for documentation/audit)
az account management-group list \
  --query "[].{name:name, displayName:displayName, parentName:details.parent.displayName, type:type}" \
  --output json > management-group-audit.json

# List all subscriptions and which management group they belong to
az account list \
  --query "[].{subscriptionId:id, name:name, managementGroupParent:managedByTenants}" \
  --output table

Management group changes take effect immediately for RBAC. For Azure Policy, the compliance scan runs every 24 hours by default, or you can trigger an on-demand scan: az policy state trigger-scan --management-group Corp
Common Mistakes and Anti-Patterns
Assigning roles to individual users instead of groups:

when you assign a role directly to a user at management group scope, every personnel change requires a manual update to the role assignment. Assign roles to Azure AD security groups instead — managing group membership is operationally simpler than managing individual role assignments.
Creating too many management groups:

more hierarchy levels mean more inheritance paths to reason about. Organizations with fewer than 20 subscriptions often need only a flat three-group structure (Prod, Non-Prod, Sandbox). Add hierarchy only when a policy or RBAC boundary genuinely differs between groups.
Putting policy assignments at the wrong scope:

a policy that should apply only to Production workloads does not belong at the Tenant Root Group. Assign it at the Corp-Prod management group scope. Over-applying policies at high scopes creates unnecessary policy violations in Sandbox environments.
Forgetting to handle the Tenant Root Group:

the root group has no default permissions for non-Global Admins. If your governance team cannot see the root group in the Azure portal, they have not been granted access to it. This is intentional but frequently causes confusion during initial setup.
Using management groups for cost allocation:

management groups do not generate separate billing statements. Cost allocation is done through subscriptions (separate invoices), tags, and Azure Cost Management scopes — not through management groups. A management group boundary is a governance boundary, not a billing boundary.
Management Groups and Microsoft Defender for Cloud

Microsoft Defender for Cloud (formerly Azure Security Center) integrates directly with management groups. When you enable Defender for Cloud at the Tenant Root Group scope, the security posture assessment covers all subscriptions in the tenant automatically — new subscriptions added to the tenant are included without requiring manual opt-in.

The Defender for Cloud dashboard aggregates Secure Score and recommendation counts across all subscriptions under the selected management group scope. You can filter by management group to show the security posture of the Corp management group separately from the Platform or Sandbox groups.

# Enable Defender for Cloud (Standard tier) across all subscriptions
# by assigning the initiative at the Tenant Root Group scope
TENANT=$(az account show --query tenantId -o tsv)

az security auto-provisioning-setting update \
  --name "MicrosoftDefenderForContainers" \
  --auto-provision "On"

# Check Defender for Cloud coverage across subscriptions under a management group
# (Use the REST API since CLI doesn't have a direct MG-scope security command)
az rest --method GET \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/Corp/providers/Microsoft.Security/assessments?api-version=2021-06-01" \
  --query "value[?properties.status.code=='Unhealthy'].{name:displayName, severity:properties.metadata.severity}" \
  --output table

# View aggregated Secure Score for a management group
az rest --method GET \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/Corp/providers/Microsoft.Security/secureScores/ascScore?api-version=2020-01-01" \
  --query "{score:properties.score.current, max:properties.score.max, percentage:properties.score.percentage}"
Summary

Azure Management Groups are the top level of the Azure resource hierarchy, providing a scope for Azure Policy and RBAC role assignments that spans multiple subscriptions. They let you govern a large subscription estate without per-subscription configuration.
The Tenant Root Group is the implicit root of every Azure AD tenant; all subscriptions are descendants of it.
Policy and RBAC assigned at a management group scope inherit to all subscriptions and resources below — a deny policy at the management group level cannot be overridden at lower scopes.
The recommended hierarchy pattern (Microsoft CAF): Tenant Root → Corp/Platform/Sandbox at level 1 → business-unit or workload-type sub-groups at level 2 → subscriptions at the leaves.
Subscriptions can be moved between management groups at any time; the move immediately changes which policies and RBAC assignments apply.

Use az account management-group create, az policy assignment create, and az role assignment create with the --scope /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/<name> parameter for CLI management.
